# Introduction


Main body text that spans the standard content width, with space on the exterior side of the page for marginal notes.

## Citing sources

Follow standard Quarto practices to cite bibliography. `_quarto.yml` configures a bibliographic source file with the line `bibliography: references.bib`. Use reference IDs to cite sources. For example, including a citation like `[@tufte2001]` will produce this citation in your text [@tufte2001]. In both HTML and PDF output, citations are linked to full bibliographic information in the [appended bibliography](./references.qmd).




https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html



## Testing the Margin Layout

This is the first sentence of the paragraph [This is your margin note content that will line up perfectly with the top line of the text block.]{.column-margin} and then the rest of your main text continues seamlessly right here in the editor.



Because we are using explicit margin blocks instead of converting all footnotes to margin notes, actual footnotes will behave completely normally.

## Standard Footnotes and Figures

Here is a sentence that requires a traditional footnote at the bottom of the page[^1]. 

[^1]: See? This footnote stays firmly anchored at the bottom of the page or screen, just like you requested.

Next, let's look at how a figure behaves. We want the caption to remain directly beneath the image rather than floating into the margin.

![A beautiful placeholder image representing structured layouts.](https://neelsmith.quarto.pub/personal/posts/imgs/filled-w-grief.png){#fig-placeholder}

As seen in @fig-placeholder, the figure spans the main text width, and its caption stays cleanly underneath.
